paragraphs.body: In the essay "What is Perception?,” Kendra Cherry expresses how complex perception is and how frequently we use it in daily life. Cherry defines perception as the process of becoming aware of objects, events, and relationships. She writes about her point by developing several important ideas. First, Cherry acknowledges the several types of perception. There is “person perception” which refers to the ability to identify and use social cues about people and relationships. There is “social perception” which is how we perceive certain societies and can be affected by things such as stereotypes. Finally, there is “selective perception” which is paying attention to some things and not others. Next, she addresses the process of perception. To simplify it, it starts with focusing on one thing to allow light to enter our eyes. This will help us focus better while electrical signals are being created. The brain begins to perceive a certain focus, and you will eventually become consciously aware. Your brain will start recognizing and interpreting the focus and you will exhibit a physical response. Finally, Cherry indicates that other outside factors influence perception. She claims that prior experiences, interests, our personalities, and how we process information can also alter the way someone may perceive life.

annotations.text: Finally, there is “selective perception” which is paying attention to some things and not others.

In the essay "What is Perception?,” Kendra Cherry expresses how complex perception is and how frequently we use it in daily life. Cherry defines perception as the process of becoming aware of objects, events, and relationships.

paragraphs.body: Cherry continues in discussing perception and how necessary it is for everyday life. I could compare perception to multiple things that I utilize daily, but don't generally spend time thinking about. For example, my water bottle, my planner, my computer, sneakers, pillows, and so many other things. For twelve years of my life, I would not be able to complete everyday activities without my grandma, or as I call her G-ma. G-ma would take me to and from school every day from the age of five in kindergarten to the age of seventeen when I got my license in my junior year of high school. She would also take me to all my after-school activities like dance, basketball, tutoring, volleyball, girl scouts, and any other event I had to be present at. My mom and dad would have to go to work early and get home late, so without G-ma there would be no way for me and my brother to get around.

annotations.text: but don't generally spend time thinking about.

paragraphs.body: I would always get to the car with a swarm of my friends and ask her on the spot "Can you take Emma, Chelsea, Elizabeth, Emily, Gia, and Maddy home?” to which she would always reply “Yes, of course.” I know she never really wanted to take all those girls home, especially when we were in middle school, but she always made sure everyone made it home safe. Those nights, I would always hear her on the phone with my mom and after their call my mom would tell me continuously how I have to stop asking G-ma these kinds of questions on the spot. I would apologize and end up doing the exact same thing a week later. Although I'm sure I annoyed G-ma those days, she never made me feel annoying or like she didn’t want to be there.

annotations.text: would apologize and end up doing the exact same thing a week later.

paragraphs.body: One thing G-ma did not do is make a left turn without a traffic light. She would go around the entirety of the town, with Frank Sinatra blasting, even if it were a street over just to avoid the left turns. Me and my brother would always complain and say how much quicker it would be to just go left, but she always refused and did her own thing. Once my brother got his license, he said how he now understood why she did that, but I still never got it. Three years later, when I began driving, I also understood why she would do that. I cannot make a left turn without thinking of her and when I have the time, I also go around the town just like she did.

annotations.text: She would go around the entirety of the town, with Frank Sinatra blasting, even if it were a street over just to avoid the left turns.

paragraphs.body: Another quirk in G-mas driving is the speed at which she would drive at. At a small side street, she would always fly down the road, but on a main road or highway she was always going thirty miles per hour. If me or my brother would comment on the speed at which she was driving, she would jokingly tell us to get out and walk and when she would take my friends home, we would have to hold in our laughter from her because of how she would drive.

The day I got my license really put everything into perspective for me. I had a parking spot at school and a car to get myself there. One of my first thoughts was about how G-ma wasn’t going to be driving me anywhere anymore. How was I going to see G-ma daily? Who was I going to rant to immediately after school? Who was going to make me laugh after a dreadful day at school? She only lives ten minutes from me, but going from seeing her every single day for twelve years to maybe once a week was a big adjustment. It made me realize not only how appreciative I am of her, but how I needed her to live my life. Similarly, our perception and senses are taken for granted daily and are in use 24/7. Imagine if one day every single one of your senses disappeared. There is no way you would be able to think or do anything, let alone complete regular daily tasks. Cherry mentions how “Through perception, we become more aware of (and can respond to) our environment.” Basically, Cherry is saying how vital perception is to stay alert of things going on around us.

paragraphs.body: I learned to appreciate G-ma while I still can even though I don’t see her as much anymore. I miss being a naive little girl who had no idea what driving meant and annoying G-ma with my futile comments. It is important to take time out of each day and appreciate stuff you would never think to appreciate. If there is one thing I’ve learned, it’s important to consider everything you have, from concepts like perception to people like G-ma, and never take it for granted because you won’t realize you had it until it’s gone.

annotations.text: It is important to take time out of each day and appreciate stuff you would never think to appreciate.
